title: Topic 297: Why Gaming During Grief Helps: Coping Through Virtual Worlds
content:
### ABSTRACT

In the esteemed annals of grief management, rarely does one find "pwning noobs" listed between "acceptance" and "a good cry." Yet, as this paper will demonstrate, the digital landscapes of video games offer a surprisingly potent balm for the bereaved soul. This research explores the seemingly paradoxical phenomenon of finding solace in virtual worlds while navigating the very real pain of loss. We will delve into the psychological underpinnings of this coping mechanism, examining how Cognitive Load Theory, the restoration of agency, and the formation of online communities contribute to a therapeutic experience. Forget five stages of grief; we propose a new model that includes at least one level-up and a boss battle. This paper will argue that, far from being a mere distraction, gaming can be a legitimate and effective tool for processing grief, proving that sometimes the best way to find yourself is to get lost in another world entirely. And maybe, just maybe, to teabag a few aliens along the way.

### INTRODUCTION

We’ve all been there. The controller is slick with sweat, your heart hammers against your ribs, and a single tear traces a path through the Cheeto dust on your cheek. Aerith is gone. Or maybe it was John Marston. Or that horse you spent 40 hours bonding with in *Red Dead Redemption 2*. The grief is real, a palpable ache for a collection of pixels and code. We mourn, we rage, we write lengthy Reddit posts. And then, we move on. But what happens when the loss isn’t a scripted event in a digital narrative, but a heart-wrenching reality in our own lives? What happens when the person we lose isn’t a character, but a loved one?

Conventional wisdom would suggest that turning to video games in a time of profound grief is, at best, a form of denial, a childish retreat into a fantasy world to avoid the painful realities of the present. The very idea of it seems absurd. "My condolences on your loss. Have you tried the new *Call of Duty*?" It’s not exactly something you’d find in a Hallmark card. And yet, a growing body of anecdotal evidence and academic research suggests that for many, video games are not just a viable coping mechanism, but a uniquely effective one. This paper will argue that video games offer a multifaceted approach to navigating the labyrinth of grief, providing not just a temporary escape, but a structured environment for processing complex emotions, a sense of control in a world that feels chaotic, and a community of fellow travelers on the often-lonely road of bereavement.

This is not to say that video games are a panacea for the pain of loss. They are not a replacement for therapy, for the comfort of friends and family, or for the simple, human need to cry. But in a world that often pathologizes grief, that sets a timetable for mourning and judges those who deviate from it, video games offer a space free from expectation. A space where you can be a hero, a villain, a farmer, or just a person quietly fishing on a digital pier. A space where you can, for a few hours, put the weight of the world on pause and simply *be*. And in the throes of grief, that is a very powerful thing indeed.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The hallowed halls of academia have, historically, been more concerned with the potential evils of video games than their therapeutic virtues. For decades, the prevailing narrative has been one of addiction, aggression, and the erosion of social skills. The image of the gamer as a pale, basement-dwelling creature, subsisting on a diet of energy drinks and social isolation, has been a persistent one. However, as gaming has become a ubiquitous part of modern culture, a more nuanced and, dare we say, more interesting conversation has begun to emerge. And in that conversation, the topic of grief has found a surprising home.

One of the most compelling theoretical frameworks for understanding the role of gaming in grief is **Cognitive Load Theory**. As Coyle (2022) explains, our cognitive capacity is finite. When we are overwhelmed by the intense emotional and cognitive demands of grief, our brains are, to put it bluntly, full. Video games, with their complex systems, demanding mechanics, and constant stream of new information, can effectively hijack our cognitive load, leaving little room for the intrusive, painful thoughts that often accompany grief. Research on the use of *Tetris* to prevent PTSD in trauma victims provides a compelling parallel, demonstrating that a cognitively demanding task can disrupt the consolidation of traumatic memories [2]. In other words, while you’re trying to figure out where to put that damn Z-block, you’re not thinking about the empty chair at the dinner table.

Beyond simple distraction, video games can also provide a sense of **agency and control** at a time when life feels utterly uncontrollable. Grief can strip us of our sense of power, leaving us feeling like helpless passengers in a life that has gone off the rails. In a video game, however, the player is in charge. They make decisions, they overcome challenges, they shape the world around them. As Iacovides, & Mekler (2019) note, this restoration of agency can be a powerful antidote to the helplessness of grief [4]. Whether it’s building a virtual farmhouse in *Stardew Valley* or leading a guild to victory in *World of Warcraft*, the act of making a meaningful choice and seeing its impact can be a profound reminder of one’s own efficacy.

Finally, the **social dimension** of gaming cannot be overstated. While the stereotype of the lonely gamer persists, the reality is that for many, gaming is a deeply social activity. Online communities, from sprawling MMOs to intimate Discord servers, can provide a vital source of support for those who are grieving. As Cole et al. (2021) found, many gamers feel more comfortable discussing their mental health with their online friends than with their offline ones [3]. In a world where grief is often a private, isolating experience, the shared camaraderie of a gaming community can be a lifeline. After all, there’s nothing quite like a well-timed resurrection from a guildmate to remind you that you’re not alone.

### ANALYSIS

So, we’ve established that video games can be more than just a way to pass the time. But how, exactly, does slaying a dragon or building a virtual city translate into a meaningful coping mechanism for grief? The answer lies in the unique ways that games engage our minds, our emotions, and our social instincts. It’s a process that is both deeply personal and surprisingly universal, a digital alchemy that transforms play into a form of therapy.

At its most basic level, gaming is a form of **structured distraction**. The world of a video game is a world of rules, of objectives, of clear and immediate feedback. This structure can be a welcome respite from the chaotic, unstructured nature of grief. When the real world feels overwhelming and unpredictable, the predictable rhythms of a game can be a source of comfort and stability. The simple act of completing a quest, of solving a puzzle, of leveling up, can provide a sense of accomplishment and forward momentum at a time when life feels stalled. It’s a small victory, to be sure, but in the face of a devastating loss, small victories are not to be underestimated.

But the therapeutic power of gaming goes far beyond mere distraction. Many games, particularly those in the role-playing genre, offer a space for **emotional exploration and expression**. In a game like *The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim*, you can be a noble hero, a dastardly villain, or anything in between. You can be brave, you can be reckless, you can be kind, you can be cruel. This freedom to experiment with different identities and emotional states can be a powerful tool for processing the complex and often contradictory emotions of grief. You can rage against the world as a barbarian, or you can quietly tend your garden as a humble farmer. You can be the person you wish you could be in the real world, or you can be the person you’re afraid to be. In the safe, consequence-free environment of a game, all of these things are possible.

Furthermore, many games are, at their core, about **overcoming adversity**. They are stories of loss, of struggle, of redemption. From the post-apocalyptic landscapes of *The Last of Us* to the whimsical, yet challenging, world of *Hollow Knight*, games are constantly asking us to face down impossible odds and emerge victorious. This narrative of resilience can be a powerful source of hope for those who are grieving. It’s a reminder that even in the darkest of times, there is always a path forward. It’s a reminder that we are stronger than we think we are. And it’s a reminder that, with enough persistence, even the biggest, baddest boss can be defeated.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The theoretical underpinnings of gaming as a grief-coping mechanism are all well and good, but where the digital rubber really meets the road is in the lived experiences of gamers themselves. The internet is awash with stories of people who have found solace, community, and a path forward through the virtual worlds of video games. These are not just isolated anecdotes; they are a testament to the profound and often-unexpected therapeutic power of play.

One of the most poignant examples of this phenomenon is the story of a young man named James, who, after the death of his father, found himself drawn back to the world of *The Legend of Zelda: Majora’s Mask* [1]. The game, which he had played with his father as a child, became a way for him to reconnect with his memories and to process his feelings of abandonment. The game’s central mechanic, a three-day cycle that repeats over and over, mirrored his own sense of being stuck in a loop of grief. But as he played, as he helped the game’s characters to resolve their own issues, he found that he was also helping himself. The game became a kind of interactive memorial, a space where he could be with his father again, and a tool for finally, a year after his death, visiting his grave.

Another powerful example can be found in the world of *Animal Crossing: New Horizons*. During the COVID-19 pandemic, when traditional funerals were impossible, many players turned to the game to create their own memorials. They built virtual graveyards, they held online services, they gathered with friends from around the world to mourn their loved ones. These virtual memorials were not a replacement for the real thing, but they were a powerful and moving way to honor the dead and to find a sense of community in a time of isolation. They were a testament to the human need to grieve, and to the creative and resourceful ways that we find to do so, even in the most challenging of circumstances.

And then there are the stories that are less about specific games and more about the communities that form around them. The story of the *Final Fantasy XIV* player who was given an in-game funeral by his guildmates after he passed away from COVID-19 is a powerful example of this. The images of hundreds of players, from all over the world, gathering to pay their respects to a fallen friend are a moving reminder of the deep and meaningful connections that can be forged in virtual worlds. These are not just "online friends"; they are people who have shared experiences, who have fought side-by-side, who have supported each other through thick and thin. And when one of them is lost, the grief is real, and the community comes together to mourn.

### DISCUSSION

The implications of all this are, to put it mildly, fascinating. For one, it challenges our long-held, and often-negative, assumptions about video games and the people who play them. The image of the gamer as a socially inept loner is, to be blunt, a load of old nonsense. The reality is that gaming is a diverse and multifaceted activity, and the people who engage in it are just as diverse and multifaceted as any other segment of the population. They are not escaping from reality; they are engaging with it in a different way. And in the context of grief, that engagement can be a powerful and positive force.

From a consumer behavior perspective, this phenomenon is a goldmine. The video game industry is already a behemoth, but the understanding that games can have a profound and positive impact on mental health opens up a whole new world of possibilities. We are already seeing the emergence of "serious games," games that are designed not just to entertain, but to educate, to heal, to promote well-being. Games like *Spiritfarer*, which explicitly deals with themes of death and dying, are at the forefront of this movement. But the potential goes far beyond that. Imagine a world where doctors could prescribe a game to help a patient cope with anxiety, or where therapists could use a virtual world to help a client process trauma. The possibilities are endless.

But perhaps the most important implication of all this is what it tells us about ourselves. It tells us that we are a resilient and resourceful species. It tells us that we will find a way to cope, to heal, to connect, no matter what life throws at us. It tells us that even in the darkest of times, we will find a way to play. And in that play, we will find a way to live. The fact that we can find solace in a world of dragons and spaceships is not a sign of our immaturity; it is a sign of our creativity, our adaptability, and our indomitable spirit. It is a sign that, no matter how much we lose, we will always find a way to keep playing.

### CONCLUSION

So, what have we learned? We’ve learned that video games, far from being a frivolous waste of time, can be a powerful and effective tool for coping with grief. We’ve learned that they can provide a much-needed distraction, a sense of control, a safe space for emotional exploration, and a community of support. We’ve learned that the line between the real world and the virtual world is not as clear as we once thought, and that the connections we make in one can have a profound impact on the other.

But perhaps the most important lesson of all is this: there is no right or wrong way to grieve. There is no timetable, there is no rulebook, there is no one-size-fits-all solution. Grief is a messy, complicated, and deeply personal journey. And if, for some of us, that journey involves a controller, a headset, and a few hours spent in a world of make-believe, then who is to say that’s a bad thing? After all, in a world that can be so cruel and so unforgiving, a little bit of make-believe might be just what we need to make it through.
references:
[1] The Guardian. (2023, April 19). ‘A space to feel at ease with dying’: how video games help people through grief. Retrieved from https://www.theguardian.com/games/2023/apr/19/how-video-games-help-people-through-grief
[2] Coyle, N. (2022, November 3). How Video Games Can Help with Grief. Psychology and Video Games. Retrieved from https://platinumparagon.info/gaming-and-grief/
[3] What's Your Grief. (2023, July 24). Video Games and Grief: an Introduction. Retrieved from https://whatsyourgrief.com/video-games-and-grief-an-introduction/
[4] Iacovides, I., & Mekler, E. D. (2019). The Role of Gaming During Difficult Life Experiences. In Proceedings of the 2019 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 1-13). ACM.
[5] Cole, J., et al. (2021). Online gaming and mental health: A study of the relationship between online gaming and mental health outcomes. Journal of Affective Disorders, 282, 103-110.
